title: The Canvas, the Game Loop & Fixed-Timestep Timing
html:
The blank slate

Every game in this track renders into a single HTML element: the <canvas>. It is a raw pixel buffer you draw into with JavaScript. Nothing happens automatically — you clear it, you draw shapes, and you do it 60 times per second yourself. That control is exactly why we use it: by the end of this track you will hand-code raycasting, pseudo-3D roads, and particle systems with no engine hiding the math.

Start with the smallest possible page. The canvas needs a width and height in pixels (the drawing buffer) which is separate from its CSS display size.

index.html
<!-- the whole page: one canvas + one module script --> <canvas id='game' width='800' height='600'></canvas> <script type='module' src='main.js'></script>

Getting a drawing context

The canvas itself draws nothing. You request a context — an object with all the drawing methods. We use the '2d' context throughout (WebGL comes in a later lesson).

main.js
// grab the element and its 2D context const canvas = document.getElementById('game'); const ctx = canvas.getContext('2d'); // draw a filled rectangle to prove it works ctx.fillStyle = '#4af'; ctx.fillRect(100, 100, 200, 80);

Why you need a loop

A game is not a still image. To animate, you must clear the screen and redraw it many times a second. The browser gives you requestAnimationFrame (rAF): you pass it a function, it calls that function right before the next screen repaint, and you call it again from inside to keep the cycle going. rAF syncs to the display refresh (usually 60Hz) and pauses in background tabs.

main.js (naive loop)
function frame() { ctx.clearRect(0, 0, canvas.width, canvas.height); // ... update and draw the world ... requestAnimationFrame(frame); // queue the next frame } requestAnimationFrame(frame); // kick it off once

The problem: frame rate varies

If you move a ball +5 pixels every frame, it travels twice as fast on a 120Hz monitor as on a 60Hz one. Worse, if a frame is slow, motion stutters. The fix is delta time (dt): measure how many seconds passed since the last frame and scale movement by it. Now x += speed * dt moves the same real-world distance regardless of frame rate.

Tip.
rAF passes your callback a high-resolution timestamp in milliseconds. Subtract the previous timestamp to get the elapsed time, then divide by 1000 for seconds.

The better fix: fixed-timestep with an accumulator

Delta time alone makes rendering framerate-independent, but variable dt wrecks physics: collision can be missed when a step is large (an object tunnels through a wall), and results stop being deterministic. The professional pattern decouples the two: run the simulation in fixed-size chunks (e.g. exactly 1/60s each) while rendering as often as the display allows. An accumulator banks leftover time between frames.

loop.js — fixed-timestep loop
const STEP = 1 / 60; // simulate at exactly 60 Hz let last = performance.now(); let acc = 0; // banked, un-simulated time function frame(now) { let dt = (now - last) / 1000; last = now; if (dt > 0.25) dt = 0.25; // clamp: avoid spiral of death acc += dt; while (acc >= STEP) { // consume time in fixed slices update(STEP); // deterministic physics step acc -= STEP; } const alpha = acc / STEP; // 0..1 leftover for interpolation render(alpha); requestAnimationFrame(frame); } requestAnimationFrame(frame);

The clamp on dt prevents the spiral of death: if a tab is hidden for 10 seconds, you do not want the while loop trying to run 600 physics steps at once. The alpha value is how far between the last two simulation states we are; you can use it to interpolate render positions for buttery-smooth motion, but a simple game can ignore it.

Law.
Update with a fixed timestep; render with whatever frame rate you get. Mixing the two is the single most common cause of physics bugs in hand-rolled games.

Update vs render

Keep two separate functions. update(dt) changes state — positions, velocities, score. render(alpha) only reads state and draws; it must never mutate the world. This separation is the backbone of every game you build from here on.

main.js — moving box
const box = { x: 0, vx: 120 }; // 120 px/sec function update(dt) { box.x += box.vx * dt; if (box.x > canvas.width - 40 || box.x < 0) box.vx *= -1; } function render() { ctx.clearRect(0, 0, canvas.width, canvas.height); ctx.fillStyle = '#4af'; ctx.fillRect(box.x, 280, 40, 40); }
Lab.
Add an on-screen FPS readout: count frames over one second and draw the number with ctx.fillText. Then drop STEP to 1/30 and watch the box still travel at the same speed — proof your timing is framerate-independent.
🎮 Build This: Core / setup — The Engine Shell

This is a CORE concept lesson, not a game genre. You are STARTING the shared engine shell that every later game (Pong, Snake, the raycaster, the racer) will plug into. Build it once, reuse it forever.

  1. Create index.html with one 800x600 canvas and a module script.
  2. In main.js, grab the 2D context and write the fixed-timestep loop with a STEP of 1/60 and a dt clamp of 0.25.
  3. Split logic into update(dt) and render() functions.
  4. Animate a box bouncing horizontally using x += vx * dt.
  5. Draw a live FPS counter so you can see frames decoupled from steps.
  6. Confirm: change STEP to 1/30 — the box speed must NOT change.

title: Clean Architecture: ES Modules, an Entity-Component-System & a State Machine
html:
Why structure matters early

A 200-line Pong clone can live in one file. A raycasting FPS cannot. If you do not impose structure now, every game becomes a tangle of globals. This lesson builds three reusable tools: ES modules to split code into files, a lightweight Entity-Component-System (ECS) to compose game objects, and a finite state machine (FSM) to manage game flow. You will import these into every game in the track.

ES Modules: one concept per file

Modern browsers run JavaScript modules natively. A file exports values; other files import them. Modules have their own scope (no accidental globals), run in strict mode, and load once even if imported many times.

math.js
// named exports export function clamp(v, lo, hi) { return v < lo ? lo : v > hi ? hi : v; } export const TAU = Math.PI * 2;
main.js
import { clamp, TAU } from './math.js'; console.log(clamp(12, 0, 10)); // 10
Danger.
Module imports require the page be served over http://, not opened as a file:// path — browsers block module loading from the filesystem. Run a tiny local server: npx serve or python3 -m http.server.

The shape of game objects

The naive approach is a deep class hierarchy: Entity → Character → Enemy → FlyingEnemy. This collapses the moment something needs traits from two branches (a flying enemy that also shoots). The ECS pattern fixes this with composition over inheritance: an entity is just an id, components are plain data bags, and systems are functions that operate on entities holding the right components.

Entities and components

Keep it minimal. An entity is an object with an id and a components map. Components carry no logic — only data.

ecs.js
export class World { constructor() { this.entities = []; // live entities this._next = 1; // id counter } create(components = {}) { const e = { id: this._next++, components }; this.entities.push(e); return e; } remove(e) { const i = this.entities.indexOf(e); if (i >= 0) this.entities.splice(i, 1); } // return entities that have ALL named components query(...names) { return this.entities.filter(e => names.every(n => n in e.components)); } }

Systems are just functions

A system queries the world for entities with the components it cares about, then updates them. Here is a movement system: it touches anything with both pos and vel, and ignores everything else.

systems.js
export function movement(world, dt) { for (const e of world.query('pos', 'vel')) { const { pos, vel } = e.components; pos.x += vel.x * dt; pos.y += vel.y * dt; } } // build an entity by composing data import { World } from './ecs.js'; const world = new World(); world.create({ pos: { x: 0, y: 0 }, vel: { x: 60, y: 0 }, sprite: { color: '#0f0' } });
Law.
Components hold data, systems hold behavior, entities hold nothing but an id and a bag of components. Keep these three responsibilities strictly separate and your code stays flexible no matter the genre.

The finite state machine

Games move between distinct modes: menu, playing, paused, game-over. Cramming this into if flags rots fast. An FSM models each mode as a state with enter, update, and exit hooks, and only one state is active at a time.

fsm.js
export class StateMachine { constructor(states) { this.states = states; // { name: { enter, update, exit } } this.current = null; } change(name, payload) { if (this.current?.exit) this.current.exit(); this.current = this.states[name]; if (this.current?.enter) this.current.enter(payload); } update(dt) { this.current?.update?.(dt); } }
using the FSM
const game = new StateMachine({ menu: { enter: () => console.log('menu'), update: (dt) => {} }, play: { enter: () => console.log('go!'), update: (dt) => movement(world, dt) }, over: { enter: (p) => console.log('score', p.score) } }); game.change('menu'); // later: game.change('over', { score: 42 });
Tip.
The FSM and ECS are orthogonal. The FSM decides which loop is running (menu vs play); the ECS decides what is in the world during the play state. Snake will use the FSM for scenes; Asteroids will lean on the ECS for bullets and rocks.
🎮 Build This: Core / architecture — The Engine Toolkit

This is a CORE concept lesson. You are EXTENDING the engine shell from p6-1 (the fixed-timestep loop) by adding the reusable architecture every later game imports. No new genre yet.

  1. Split your project into modules: math.js, ecs.js, fsm.js, systems.js, main.js.
  2. Serve over http:// with npx serve so module imports work.
  3. Implement the World class with create, remove, and query.
  4. Write a movement system and spawn a few entities with pos/vel components.
  5. Build the StateMachine and wire menu, play, and over states.
  6. In your loop, call fsm.update(dt) from update; press a key to switch from menu to play.

title: Input In Depth: Keyboard, Mouse, Touch & Gamepad
html:
Raw events are not enough

Browsers fire keydown when a key first presses and then repeats it on a timer — useless for smooth movement. Mouse coordinates arrive in page space, not canvas space. Touch and gamepad have entirely different APIs. If every game reads raw events directly, you rewrite the same plumbing each time and bind controls inconsistently. The fix is one input manager that maps physical inputs to abstract actions like 'left', 'fire', 'jump' — rebindable and device-agnostic.

State, not events

Games poll input once per frame; they do not react to events mid-frame. So the manager listens to events but only records state (is this key currently down?). Your game asks input.isDown('fire') during update. We also track edges: justPressed (down this frame, up last frame) is what you want for menu navigation and single shots.

input.js — keyboard core
export class Input { constructor(bindings) { this.bindings = bindings; // { left: ['ArrowLeft','KeyA'], ... } this.down = new Set(); // physical codes held now this.prev = new Set(); // snapshot from last frame addEventListener('keydown', e => { this.down.add(e.code); // e.code is layout-independent }); addEventListener('keyup', e => this.down.delete(e.code)); } // is ANY key bound to this action currently held? isDown(action) { return (this.bindings[action] || []).some(c => this.down.has(c)); } justPressed(action) { return (this.bindings[action] || []).some(c => this.down.has(c) && !this.prev.has(c)); } // call at the END of every frame to advance edges postUpdate() { this.prev = new Set(this.down); } }
Tip.
Bind to e.code (physical key, e.g. 'KeyW') not e.key (the produced character, which changes with layout and Shift). The W key is 'KeyW' on QWERTY, AZERTY, and Dvorak alike.

Mouse: convert to canvas space

A mouse event reports clientX/clientY relative to the viewport. To know where that is on your canvas, subtract the canvas's bounding rectangle and scale by the ratio of the drawing buffer to the displayed size.

input.js — mouse
attachMouse(canvas) { this.mouse = { x: 0, y: 0, left: false }; const toCanvas = (e) => { const r = canvas.getBoundingClientRect(); this.mouse.x = (e.clientX - r.left) * (canvas.width / r.width); this.mouse.y = (e.clientY - r.top) * (canvas.height / r.height); }; canvas.addEventListener('mousemove', toCanvas); canvas.addEventListener('mousedown', e => { toCanvas(e); this.mouse.left = true; }); addEventListener('mouseup', () => { this.mouse.left = false; }); }
Law.
Never trust that canvas pixels equal CSS pixels. Always scale mouse coordinates by canvas.width / rect.width. The raycaster and twin-stick shooter will aim with the mouse and break instantly if you skip this.

Touch: synthesize a virtual stick

On phones there is no keyboard. The common pattern is an on-screen virtual joystick: the first touch sets an origin, and the vector from origin to current finger position becomes a direction. Map that vector onto your movement actions.

input.js — touch stick
attachTouch(canvas) { this.stick = { x: 0, y: 0 }; // -1..1 each axis let origin = null; canvas.addEventListener('touchstart', e => { const t = e.touches[0]; origin = { x: t.clientX, y: t.clientY }; }); canvas.addEventListener('touchmove', e => { const t = e.touches[0]; const dx = t.clientX - origin.x, dy = t.clientY - origin.y; const max = 50; // px to full deflection this.stick.x = Math.max(-1, Math.min(1, dx / max)); this.stick.y = Math.max(-1, Math.min(1, dy / max)); e.preventDefault(); // stop page scrolling }, { passive: false }); canvas.addEventListener('touchend', () => { this.stick = { x: 0, y: 0 }; }); }

Gamepad: polled, not evented

The Gamepad API is unusual — there are no events for button presses. You poll navigator.getGamepads() every frame and read the live snapshot. Axes are floats in -1..1 (apply a dead zone to ignore stick drift); buttons have a pressed boolean and an analog value.

input.js — gamepad poll
pollPad() { const pad = navigator.getGamepads()[0]; if (!pad) return; const dz = 0.15; // dead zone const ax = pad.axes[0], ay = pad.axes[1]; this.padX = Math.abs(ax) > dz ? ax : 0; this.padY = Math.abs(ay) > dz ? ay : 0; this.padFire = pad.buttons[0].pressed; // A / cross }
Lab.
Make bindings rebindable: add a rebind(action) method that captures the next keydown and overwrites that action's binding. Save the bindings object to localStorage so custom controls survive a reload.

One unified read

The game should not care where input came from. Combine sources behind the action API: isDown('left') returns true if the A key, the left arrow, the touch stick, OR the gamepad stick says left. That single abstraction is what makes the same game playable on desktop, phone, and controller.

🎮 Build This: Core / input — The Input Manager

This is a CORE concept lesson. You are EXTENDING the engine (loop from p6-1, modules from p6-2) with the input layer every later game relies on. No new genre.

  1. Create input.js with the Input class and a default bindings map (left, right, up, down, fire).
  2. Track down/prev sets; expose isDown and justPressed.
  3. Attach mouse (canvas-space coords), a touch virtual stick, and gamepad polling.
  4. Merge all sources so isDown('left') works from keyboard, touch, or pad.
  5. Call input.postUpdate() at the end of each frame to advance edge detection.
  6. Add rebind(action) and persist bindings to localStorage.

title: Vector Math & Pong
html:
Why vectors first

Almost every game quantity — position, velocity, the direction a bullet flies, the normal of a wall — is a 2D vector: an (x, y) pair. Learn five operations and you can express nearly all 2D game logic: add, scale, dot, normalize, and reflect. We will build them, then immediately spend them building Pong, where the ball's bounce is literally a vector reflection.

The five operations

OperationWhat it doesUsed for
addcombine two vectorsmove position by velocity
scalemultiply by a numberapply speed, dt
dota·b = ax·bx + ay·byangles, projection, reflect
normalizeshrink to length 1pure direction
reflectbounce across a normalball off paddle/wall
vec.js
export const add = (a, b) => ({ x: a.x + b.x, y: a.y + b.y }); export const scale = (a, s) => ({ x: a.x * s, y: a.y * s }); export const dot = (a, b) => a.x * b.x + a.y * b.y; export const len = (a) => Math.hypot(a.x, a.y); export function normalize(a) { const l = len(a) || 1; // avoid divide by zero return { x: a.x / l, y: a.y / l }; } // reflect velocity v across a unit normal n: v - 2(v.n)n export function reflect(v, n) { const d = dot(v, n) * 2; return { x: v.x - d * n.x, y: v.y - d * n.y }; }
Tip.
The dot product has a beautiful meaning: a·b = |a||b|cosθ. When both are unit length, the dot product IS the cosine of the angle between them — positive means roughly same direction, zero means perpendicular, negative means opposing.

Reflection is the heart of bouncing

When the ball hits a flat wall, only the component along the wall's normal flips. For a vertical wall (left/right of screen) the normal is horizontal, so you flip vx. For the top/bottom it is vertical, so you flip vy. The general reflect formula handles any normal, which matters when we make the paddle angle the bounce.

Building Pong: the entities

Two paddles and one ball. Each is a simple object. The ball stores velocity as a vector; speed is the vector's length.

pong.js — setup
const W = 800, H = 600, PH = 90, PW = 12; const left = { x: 24, y: H / 2 - PH / 2, score: 0 }; const right = { x: W - 36, y: H / 2 - PH / 2, score: 0 }; const ball = { x: W / 2, y: H / 2, vx: 260, vy: 120, r: 8 }; const PADDLE_SPEED = 420;

Updating with delta time

Reuse the fixed-timestep loop from p6-1. The left paddle follows the player; the right is a simple AI that eases toward the ball's y. The ball integrates its velocity, bounces off the top and bottom walls, and resets when it leaves the left or right edge.

pong.js — update
function update(dt) { // player paddle if (input.isDown('up')) left.y -= PADDLE_SPEED * dt; if (input.isDown('down')) left.y += PADDLE_SPEED * dt; left.y = Math.max(0, Math.min(H - PH, left.y)); // AI paddle eases toward the ball const target = ball.y - PH / 2; right.y += (target - right.y) * Math.min(1, 6 * dt); // integrate ball ball.x += ball.vx * dt; ball.y += ball.vy * dt; // top / bottom walls: flip vy (reflect off horizontal normal) if (ball.y - ball.r < 0) { ball.y = ball.r; ball.vy = Math.abs(ball.vy); } if (ball.y + ball.r > H) { ball.y = H - ball.r; ball.vy = -Math.abs(ball.vy); } collidePaddle(left, 1); collidePaddle(right, -1); checkScore(); }

Paddle physics: spin from contact point

A flat bounce is boring. Real Pong gives the player control: where the ball strikes the paddle changes the angle. Compute how far from the paddle center the ball hit (-1 at top, +1 at bottom), use that to set vy, and keep total speed constant by recomputing from the angle. The dir argument sends the ball back the correct horizontal way.

pong.js — paddle bounce
function collidePaddle(p, dir) { const hit = ball.x + ball.r * -dir > p.x && ball.x - ball.r * -dir < p.x + PW && ball.y > p.y && ball.y < p.y + PH; if (!hit) return; // where on the paddle did we hit? -1 top .. +1 bottom const rel = ((ball.y - p.y) / PH) * 2 - 1; const speed = Math.hypot(ball.vx, ball.vy) * 1.04; // speed up const angle = rel * 0.9; // up to ~50 degrees ball.vx = dir * speed * Math.cos(angle); ball.vy = speed * Math.sin(angle); }
Law.
Always nudge the ball back outside the paddle (or flip its direction before integrating) so it cannot get stuck inside on the next frame. A ball lodged in a paddle is the classic beginner Pong bug.

Render and score

Rendering is trivial: clear, draw two rectangles and a circle (or square) ball, then the scores with fillText. On a miss, recenter the ball and serve it toward the player who scored.

pong.js — score & render
function checkScore() { if (ball.x < 0) { right.score++; serve(-1); } if (ball.x > W) { left.score++; serve(1); } } function serve(dir) { ball.x = W / 2; ball.y = H / 2; ball.vx = dir * 260; ball.vy = (Math.random() * 2 - 1) * 160; } function render() { ctx.fillStyle = '#000'; ctx.fillRect(0, 0, W, H); ctx.fillStyle = '#fff'; ctx.fillRect(left.x, left.y, PW, PH); ctx.fillRect(right.x, right.y, PW, PH); ctx.fillRect(ball.x - ball.r, ball.y - ball.r, ball.r * 2, ball.r * 2); ctx.font = '32px monospace'; ctx.fillText(left.score, W / 2 - 60, 40); ctx.fillText(right.score, W / 2 + 40, 40); }
🎮 Build This: Classic arcade — Pong

This STARTS a new genre: classic arcade. Pong is the first real game in the track — it uses the loop (p6-1), modules (p6-2), and input (p6-3) you already built.

  1. Write vec.js with add, scale, dot, normalize, and reflect.
  2. Set up two paddles and a ball with a velocity vector.
  3. Move the player paddle with input; ease an AI paddle toward the ball.
  4. Integrate the ball by dt; bounce off top and bottom walls.
  5. Implement paddle collision where the contact point sets the bounce angle and speed ramps up slightly each hit.
  6. Add scoring, recenter-and-serve, and draw the scoreboard with fillText.

title: AABB Collision & Breakout
html:
The most useful collision test in 2D

An axis-aligned bounding box (AABB) is a rectangle whose edges line up with the x and y axes — no rotation. Almost everything in arcade games can be approximated by one: paddles, bricks, ships, pickups. Two AABBs overlap when they overlap on BOTH axes simultaneously. That is the entire test, and it is four comparisons.

aabb.js
// boxes as { x, y, w, h }, x/y is the top-left corner export function intersects(a, b) { return a.x < b.x + b.w && a.x + a.w > b.x && a.y < b.y + b.h && a.y + a.h > b.y; }
Law.
AABB overlap = overlap on the x-axis AND overlap on the y-axis. If a gap exists on either axis, the boxes cannot be touching. This is the separating-axis idea in its simplest form.

Detection is easy; resolution is the craft

Knowing two boxes overlap is half the job. You also must push them apart correctly and decide which way the ball bounces. The trick is to find the minimum translation — the smallest move that separates them. Compute the overlap on each axis; whichever overlap is smaller is the axis you collided on, so you resolve along that axis and flip the matching velocity component.

aabb.js — resolve
// move ball out of brick along the shallow axis, return axis hit export function resolve(ball, brick) { // signed overlaps on each side const overlapX = Math.min(ball.x + ball.w, brick.x + brick.w) - Math.max(ball.x, brick.x); const overlapY = Math.min(ball.y + ball.h, brick.y + brick.h) - Math.max(ball.y, brick.y); if (overlapX < overlapY) { // shallower on x => hit a vertical face const sign = ball.x < brick.x ? -1 : 1; ball.x += sign * overlapX; return 'x'; } else { const sign = ball.y < brick.y ? -1 : 1; ball.y += sign * overlapY; return 'y'; } }
Tip.
Resolving on the shallow axis is why a ball that clips the corner of a brick bounces sensibly instead of teleporting through. Always separate first, then flip the velocity on the axis you separated along.

Building the brick grid

A 2D array describes the level. Each non-zero cell becomes a brick whose value is its hit points (multi-hit bricks). We lay them out with simple arithmetic from row and column indices.

breakout.js — bricks
const COLS = 10, ROWS = 6, BW = 72, BH = 24, GAP = 6; const bricks = []; for (let r = 0; r < ROWS; r++) { for (let c = 0; c < COLS; c++) { bricks.push({ x: 30 + c * (BW + GAP), y: 60 + r * (BH + GAP), w: BW, h: BH, hp: r < 2 ? 2 : 1, // top rows take two hits alive: true }); } }

Ball vs bricks each frame

The ball reuses Pong's paddle bounce. Each frame, after integrating, test the ball against every live brick. On a hit: resolve position, flip the right velocity component, decrement hp, and drop a power-up sometimes.

breakout.js — collisions
function ballVsBricks() { for (const brick of bricks) { if (!brick.alive || !intersects(ball, brick)) continue; const axis = resolve(ball, brick); if (axis === 'x') ball.vx *= -1; else ball.vy *= -1; brick.hp--; if (brick.hp <= 0) { brick.alive = false; score += 10; if (Math.random() < 0.15) dropPowerUp(brick.x, brick.y); } break; // one brick per frame avoids double-flips } }
Danger.
If you bounce off two bricks in one frame you may flip vy twice and cancel the bounce, sending the ball straight through. Resolving only the first overlapping brick per frame (the break) is a simple, robust fix for a grid.

Power-ups

A power-up is a small falling AABB. When it reaches the paddle, apply an effect. Classic options: widen the paddle, multiball, or a slower ball. Model them as entities that fall and get tested against the paddle.

breakout.js — power-ups
const powerups = []; function dropPowerUp(x, y) { const kinds = ['wide', 'slow', 'multi']; powerups.push({ x, y, w: 20, h: 20, vy: 150, kind: kinds[(Math.random() * kinds.length) | 0] }); } function updatePowerUps(dt) { for (let i = powerups.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) { const p = powerups[i]; p.y += p.vy * dt; if (intersects(p, paddle)) { apply(p.kind); powerups.splice(i, 1); } else if (p.y > H) powerups.splice(i, 1); } } function apply(kind) { if (kind === 'wide') paddle.w = Math.min(200, paddle.w + 40); if (kind === 'slow') { ball.vx *= 0.8; ball.vy *= 0.8; } // 'multi' would spawn an extra ball entity }
Lab.
Tint multi-hit bricks by remaining hp (e.g. red at hp 2, orange at hp 1) so the player can read the level. Then add a win check: when no brick is alive, switch the FSM to a win state.
🎮 Build This: Classic arcade — Breakout

This is classic arcade and it EXTENDS Pong (p6-4): keep that ball, paddle, and paddle-angle bounce, then add a brick grid to make a new game, Breakout.

  1. Write aabb.js with intersects and the shallow-axis resolve.
  2. Build a brick grid from a ROWS x COLS layout with per-brick hp.
  3. Reuse the Pong paddle bounce; keep the ball above the bottom edge or lose a life.
  4. Each frame, resolve the ball against the first overlapping brick and flip the correct velocity axis.
  5. Decrement hp, score on destroy, and tint bricks by remaining hp.
  6. Drop falling power-ups (wide / slow / multi) and apply them when the paddle catches one.

title: Grid Logic, Scenes & Snake
html:
A different kind of movement

Pong and Breakout move continuously in pixels. Snake moves in discrete steps on a grid — the snake jumps one cell at a time on a timer, not a smooth slide. Grid logic underpins a huge family of games (roguelikes, match-3, tower defense), so it is worth doing cleanly. We will also build a scene manager on top of the FSM from p6-2 to handle menu → play → game-over, and persist a high score with localStorage.

The grid and cell coordinates

Decide a cell size in pixels, then think in cells everywhere except rendering. A cell at column cx, row cy draws at pixel cx * CELL, cy * CELL. The snake is an array of cells, head first; food is a single cell.

snake.js — grid setup
const CELL = 24; const COLS = 25, ROWS = 25; // 600x600 board let snake = [{ x: 12, y: 12 }]; // cells, head at index 0 let dir = { x: 1, y: 0 }; // moving right let nextDir = { x: 1, y: 0 }; // buffered input let food = { x: 5, y: 5 }; let score = 0;

Stepping on a timer, not every frame

If the snake moved every frame it would be a blur. Instead accumulate time and advance one cell every stepTime seconds (which shrinks as the snake grows, raising difficulty). This is a second, coarser timer layered over the fixed-timestep loop.

snake.js — stepped movement
let timer = 0; let stepTime = 0.12; // seconds per cell function updatePlay(dt) { timer += dt; if (timer < stepTime) return; // not time to move yet timer -= stepTime; stepSnake(); }

Buffering direction to avoid suicide

Two rules: the snake cannot reverse directly into itself, and we must buffer the input. If the player taps up-then-left within one step, both should register in order. We store nextDir from input but only commit it at the moment of stepping, rejecting a 180-degree turn.

snake.js — input & step
function readDir() { if (input.justPressed('up') && dir.y === 0) nextDir = { x: 0, y: -1 }; if (input.justPressed('down') && dir.y === 0) nextDir = { x: 0, y: 1 }; if (input.justPressed('left') && dir.x === 0) nextDir = { x: -1, y: 0 }; if (input.justPressed('right') && dir.x === 0) nextDir = { x: 1, y: 0 }; } function stepSnake() { dir = nextDir; // commit buffered turn const head = { x: snake[0].x + dir.x, y: snake[0].y + dir.y }; // wall collision = death if (head.x < 0 || head.x >= COLS || head.y < 0 || head.y >= ROWS) return die(); // self collision = death if (snake.some(s => s.x === head.x && s.y === head.y)) return die(); snake.unshift(head); // grow forward if (head.x === food.x && head.y === food.y) { score += 1; stepTime = Math.max(0.05, stepTime - 0.004); // speed up placeFood(); } else { snake.pop(); // no food: drop the tail (constant length) } }
Law.
Grow the snake by adding a head and (when no food was eaten) removing the tail. Never resize the whole array — push the head with unshift, drop the tail with pop. Eating simply skips the pop.

Placing food off the snake

Food must not spawn under the snake. Pick random cells until you find a free one. For a near-full board you would build a list of free cells instead, but rejection sampling is fine here.

snake.js — food
function placeFood() { do { food = { x: (Math.random() * COLS) | 0, y: (Math.random() * ROWS) | 0 }; } while (snake.some(s => s.x === food.x && s.y === food.y)); }

Scenes with the FSM

Reuse the StateMachine from p6-2. Three states: menu waits for a key and shows the high score; play runs the game; over shows the result and offers a restart. The scene that is active decides what update and render do.

snake.js — scenes & high score
const HS_KEY = 'snake.highscore'; let high = Number(localStorage.getItem(HS_KEY)) || 0; const scenes = new StateMachine({ menu: { update: () => { if (input.justPressed('fire')) scenes.change('play'); } }, play: { enter: () => { snake = [{ x: 12, y: 12 }]; dir = { x: 1, y: 0 }; nextDir = dir; score = 0; stepTime = 0.12; placeFood(); }, update: (dt) => { readDir(); updatePlay(dt); } }, over: { update: () => { if (input.justPressed('fire')) scenes.change('play'); } } }); scenes.change('menu'); function die() { if (score > high) { high = score; localStorage.setItem(HS_KEY, high); } scenes.change('over'); }
Tip.
localStorage only stores strings. Wrap reads in Number(...) or JSON.parse(...) and writes in String(...) or JSON.stringify(...). For a whole save object, JSON.stringify the object and parse it back on load.
Lab.
Add a subtle render polish: draw the snake head a different color, and ease each segment toward its target cell using the leftover step time as an interpolation factor for smooth motion between grid jumps.
🎮 Build This: Classic arcade — Snake

This STARTS a new game in the classic arcade genre: Snake. It introduces grid movement and a full scene/high-score flow, reusing the FSM from p6-2 and input from p6-3.

  1. Define a grid (CELL size, COLS, ROWS) and store the snake as an array of cells.
  2. Move one cell every stepTime seconds using a timer accumulator.
  3. Buffer direction with justPressed and reject 180-degree turns.
  4. Grow with unshift(head); pop() the tail unless food was eaten.
  5. Detect wall and self collisions for death; speed up as the snake grows.
  6. Wire menu / play / over scenes with the FSM and persist the high score to localStorage.

title: Wrapping Worlds, Rotation & Asteroids
html:
From grid to free rotation

Asteroids drops the grid entirely. The ship points anywhere through 360 degrees, thrusts in its facing direction with momentum, and the world wraps so leaving one edge enters the opposite. This lesson is your trigonometry workout: sin and cos turn an angle into a direction vector, and circle-vs-circle distance checks replace AABBs because rotated rocks are not axis-aligned.

Angles to direction

Store the ship's facing as an angle in radians. The unit vector pointing that way is (cosθ, sinθ). In canvas, y grows downward, so this naturally treats angle 0 as pointing right; rotating clockwise increases the angle. Thrust adds a scaled facing vector to velocity; the ship keeps drifting when you let go (Newton's first law — there is no friction in space, though we add a tiny drag for playability).

asteroids.js — the ship
const ship = { x: W / 2, y: H / 2, vx: 0, vy: 0, angle: -Math.PI / 2, // pointing up r: 14 }; const TURN = 3.2; // radians/sec const THRUST = 240; // px/sec^2 const DRAG = 0.6; // gentle slowdown per sec
asteroids.js — ship update
function updateShip(dt) { if (input.isDown('left')) ship.angle -= TURN * dt; if (input.isDown('right')) ship.angle += TURN * dt; if (input.isDown('up')) { ship.vx += Math.cos(ship.angle) * THRUST * dt; ship.vy += Math.sin(ship.angle) * THRUST * dt; } // drag: scale velocity slightly toward zero ship.vx -= ship.vx * DRAG * dt; ship.vy -= ship.vy * DRAG * dt; ship.x += ship.vx * dt; ship.y += ship.vy * dt; wrap(ship); }
Tip.
Thrust changes velocity, not position. That one indirection is what gives Asteroids its signature floaty inertia: you accelerate in the direction you face but coast in the direction you were already moving.

Screen wrapping

When any entity crosses an edge, teleport it to the opposite side. Use the modulo of the world dimensions; adding the dimension before the modulo keeps negatives positive.

asteroids.js — wrap
function wrap(o) { if (o.x < 0) o.x += W; else if (o.x > W) o.x -= W; if (o.y < 0) o.y += H; else if (o.y > H) o.y -= H; }
Law.
Wrapping means an object near an edge is logically near the opposite edge too. For pixel-perfect visuals you would draw the ship a second time offset by the world size; for gameplay collision, comparing wrapped centers is enough.

Firing bullets

A bullet spawns at the ship's nose, inherits a velocity along the ship's facing (plus the ship's own velocity for realism), and dies after a short lifetime. Store bullets in an array and cull dead ones each frame.

asteroids.js — bullets
const bullets = []; function fire() { const c = Math.cos(ship.angle), s = Math.sin(ship.angle); bullets.push({ x: ship.x + c * ship.r, y: ship.y + s * ship.r, vx: ship.vx + c * 520, vy: ship.vy + s * 520, r: 2, life: 1.1 // seconds }); } function updateBullets(dt) { for (let i = bullets.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) { const b = bullets[i]; b.x += b.vx * dt; b.y += b.vy * dt; wrap(b); b.life -= dt; if (b.life <= 0) bullets.splice(i, 1); } }

Circle collision and splitting

Rocks are irregular but we collide them as circles: two circles overlap when the distance between centers is less than the sum of radii. To avoid a square root, compare squared distance to the squared radius sum.

asteroids.js — circle hit
function hit(a, b) { const dx = a.x - b.x, dy = a.y - b.y; const rr = a.r + b.r; return dx * dx + dy * dy < rr * rr; // no sqrt needed }

When a bullet hits an asteroid, remove both. A large asteroid splits into two smaller ones flung in random directions; the smallest just vanishes. Score scales with how small the rock was.

asteroids.js — collisions & split
function spawnAsteroid(x, y, size) { const a = Math.random() * Math.PI * 2; const spd = 40 + Math.random() * 60; asteroids.push({ x, y, vx: Math.cos(a) * spd, vy: Math.sin(a) * spd, r: size, size }); // size: 40 big, 22 med, 12 small } function bulletsVsRocks() { for (let i = asteroids.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) { for (let j = bullets.length - 1; j >= 0; j--) { if (!hit(asteroids[i], bullets[j])) continue; const rock = asteroids[i]; bullets.splice(j, 1); asteroids.splice(i, 1); score += 100 - rock.size; if (rock.size > 14) { const next = rock.size === 40 ? 22 : 12; spawnAsteroid(rock.x, rock.y, next); spawnAsteroid(rock.x, rock.y, next); } break; } } }
Lab.
Render the ship as a triangle using ctx.save, ctx.translate(ship.x, ship.y), ctx.rotate(ship.angle), then draw a triangle pointing right and ctx.restore. When thrusting, draw a flickering flame behind it for feedback.
🎮 Build This: Classic arcade — Asteroids

This STARTS a new game in the classic arcade genre: Asteroids. It brings rotation, thrust-based inertia, screen wrapping, and circle collision together for the first time.

  1. Give the ship an angle; turn with input and thrust along (cos, sin) into velocity with gentle drag.
  2. Wrap the ship, bullets, and asteroids around screen edges with modulo math.
  3. Fire bullets from the ship's nose that inherit ship velocity and expire after a lifetime.
  4. Collide everything as circles using squared-distance comparisons.
  5. Split large asteroids into two smaller ones on hit; smallest disappears; score by size.
  6. End the game if an asteroid hits the ship; render the ship with save/rotate/restore.

title: Spritesheet Animation & Space Invaders
html:
From shapes to images

So far everything has been rectangles and circles. Real games draw sprites — images. Loading one image per frame would be wasteful, so games pack many frames into a single spritesheet and blit sub-rectangles of it. This lesson covers loading images, slicing a sheet, driving frame-based animation off a timer, and then builds Space Invaders, whose marching aliens are pure spritesheet animation.

Loading images (asynchronously)

An Image loads over the network, so you must wait for its onload before drawing it. Wrapping loads in promises lets you await all assets before starting the game.

assets.js
export function loadImage(src) { return new Promise((resolve, reject) => { const img = new Image(); img.onload = () => resolve(img); img.onerror = reject; img.src = src; // kicks off the load }); } // await all assets, THEN start the game const sheet = await loadImage('invaders.png'); startGame(sheet);
Danger.
Drawing an image before it finishes loading silently renders nothing (or throws in older browsers). Always start your game inside the load completion, never assume an image is ready on the line after you set its src.

Slicing a spritesheet

The 9-argument form of drawImage copies a source rectangle from the sheet to a destination rectangle on the canvas: drawImage(img, sx, sy, sw, sh, dx, dy, dw, dh). If frames are laid out in a uniform grid, the source x/y are just frame * frameW and row * frameH.

sprite.js
// draw one frame of a horizontal strip export function drawFrame(ctx, sheet, frame, fw, fh, dx, dy) { ctx.drawImage( sheet, frame * fw, 0, fw, fh, // source rect in sheet dx, dy, fw, fh // destination on canvas ); }
Tip.
For crisp pixel art, set ctx.imageSmoothingEnabled = false. The browser's default bilinear smoothing blurs upscaled retro sprites; turning it off keeps hard pixel edges.

Frame-based animation

Animation cycles frames on a timer, independent of frame rate. Accumulate dt; when it exceeds the per-frame duration, advance the frame index and wrap with modulo. Space Invaders aliens have just two frames that alternate — a tiny but iconic animation.

sprite.js — animator
export class Anim { constructor(frameCount, fps) { this.count = frameCount; this.frameTime = 1 / fps; this.t = 0; this.frame = 0; } update(dt) { this.t += dt; if (this.t >= this.frameTime) { this.t -= this.frameTime; this.frame = (this.frame + 1) % this.count; } } }

The alien grid

Invaders are a grid that moves as one block: the whole formation shuffles sideways, and when any alien touches the edge the entire grid drops a row and reverses direction. Track formation position and direction, not per-alien velocity.

invaders.js — formation
const COLS = 11, ROWS = 5, AW = 32, AH = 24, GAP = 16; const aliens = []; for (let r = 0; r < ROWS; r++) for (let c = 0; c < COLS; c++) aliens.push({ col: c, row: r, alive: true }); let formX = 40, formY = 60, formDir = 1, formSpeed = 30; const anim = new Anim(2, 2); // 2 frames, slow shuffle
invaders.js — march
function updateAliens(dt) { anim.update(dt); formX += formDir * formSpeed * dt; // bounds of the living formation const live = aliens.filter(a => a.alive); const cols = live.map(a => a.col); const minX = formX + Math.min(...cols) * (AW + GAP); const maxX = formX + Math.max(...cols) * (AW + GAP) + AW; if (maxX > W - 20 || minX < 20) { formDir *= -1; // reverse formY += 16; // drop a row formSpeed += 4; // fewer aliens = faster (tension!) } }
Law.
In Space Invaders the formation speeds up as aliens die — fewer entities to step means each step covers more ground. That escalating pressure is the game's core feel; preserve it by raising formSpeed on each edge bounce or kill.

Player, bullets and destructible shields

The player moves left/right and fires upward (usually one bullet on screen at a time, the original limit). Aliens drop bombs at random. Shields are blocks of small destructible cells: any bullet that hits a cell removes it, carving holes over time.

invaders.js — shields & hits
// each shield is a grid of small destructible cells function makeShield(ox, oy) { const cells = []; for (let y = 0; y < 6; y++) for (let x = 0; x < 10; x++) cells.push({ x: ox + x * 6, y: oy + y * 6, w: 6, h: 6 }); return cells; } // bullet vs shield: remove the first overlapping cell function bulletVsShield(b, cells) { for (let i = 0; i < cells.length; i++) { if (intersects(b, cells[i])) { cells.splice(i, 1); return true; } } return false; }

Reuse the intersects AABB test from Breakout for every bullet-vs-target check. Rendering ties it together: draw each living alien with drawFrame(ctx, sheet, anim.frame, AW, AH, ...) so the whole formation animates in lockstep.

Lab.
Give different rows different alien sprites (different source rows in the sheet) and score values. Add the occasional bonus UFO that slides across the top for big points, and play a marching beat that speeds up with formSpeed.
🎮 Build This: Classic arcade — Space Invaders

This STARTS a new game in the classic arcade genre: Space Invaders. It introduces image loading and spritesheet animation, reusing the AABB intersects from Breakout (p6-5).

  1. Write loadImage (promise-based) and await your spritesheet before starting.
  2. Slice frames with the 9-argument drawImage; disable smoothing for crisp pixels.
  3. Build the Anim class and animate the alien formation's two frames.
  4. March the grid sideways, drop and reverse at edges, and speed up as aliens die.
  5. Add a player with a single upward bullet and random alien bombs, all collided with intersects.
  6. Add destructible shields made of small cells that bullets carve away; render living aliens with drawFrame.
